title: Why Relaxation Feels Threatening After Complex Trauma
researcherTopic: yoga and consciousness, the mechanics of the mind
metaDescription: For survivors of complex trauma, relaxation can feel like a threat. Discover why your nervous system resists safety and how to gently guide it toward rest.
imageDescription: A delicate, handcrafted ceramic bowl, repaired with shimmering gold in the kintsugi style, rests on a rustic wooden surface. Warm, gentle morning light illuminates the intricate golden cracks, symbolizing the beauty and strength found in healing. The scene evokes a sense of peace, resilience, and the quiet dignity of a mended past.
htmlContent:
Nobody warns you that healing complex trauma means the nervous system must unlearn the very strategies that kept it alive. The hypervigilance that scans every room for threats, the bracing for impact that lives in the shoulders, the shallow breath that keeps you ready to flee—these were not mistakes. They were adaptations. And so, when genuine safety finally arrives, when the world softens and the possibility of rest presents itself, the body does not rejoice. It panics. Because for a nervous system wired for danger, the absence of a threat feels like the most terrifying threat of all. It is the silence after the bomb drops, the eerie calm before the storm. It is the feeling of being exposed, unguarded, and utterly unprepared for the other shoe that has always, eventually, dropped.

Most of what passes for healing is just rearranging the furniture in a burning house.

The Architecture of Bracing

For those who have navigated the treacherous terrain of complex trauma, the body becomes a fortress. Every muscle, every nerve fiber is recruited into a state of perpetual readiness. This is not a conscious choice; it is a deeply ingrained physiological reality. The nervous system, in its profound and ancient wisdom, has learned that the world is not a safe place. It has learned that danger can erupt at any moment, from any direction. And so, it builds a fortress of tension, a suit of armor forged in the fires of past experience. This armor is heavy, it is exhausting, but it is also familiar. It is, in a strange and painful way, home.

When we attempt to relax, we are, in essence, asking the body to dismantle this fortress. We are asking it to lay down its weapons, to open the gates, and to trust that the world outside is no longer a battlefield. But the body remembers. It remembers the ambushes, the betrayals, the moments of sheer terror when its defenses were breached. And so, it resists. It tightens. It floods the system with alarm signals. Relaxation, from this perspective, is not a release. It is a profound and existential threat. It is a vulnerability that the nervous system has learned, through bitter experience, it cannot afford.

A client once described this to me as feeling like a tightly coiled spring. “For years,” she said, “that coil was the only thing that held me together. The thought of letting it go, of unspooling… it feels like I would simply fall apart.” This is the paradox of healing. The very tension that causes so much suffering is also the scaffolding that has kept us upright. To let go of the tension is to let go of the only structure we have ever known. It is to step into a vast and terrifying emptiness, without any guarantee that we will find solid ground on the other side. We must learn to build a new kind of structure, one based not on tension, but on a felt sense of internal safety. A structure that can bend without breaking, that can yield without collapsing. This is the slow, patient work of rebuilding the foundations of the self.

The Tyranny of the Vagus Nerve

The vagus nerve, that great wandering nerve that connects the brain to the body, is the superhighway of our felt experience. It is the primary channel through which the brain and the gut communicate, the pathway along which the sensations of safety and danger travel. In a healthy, well-regulated nervous system, the vagus nerve operates with a graceful and fluid rhythm, shifting between states of activation and rest with ease. But in a nervous system shaped by complex trauma, this rhythm is broken. The vagus nerve becomes, in effect, a tyrant, locking the body into a state of chronic hyperarousal or hypoarousal.

Richard Schwartz's introduction to Internal Family Systems is transformative No Bad Parts by Richard Schwartz (paid link).

When we are in a state of hyperarousal, the sympathetic branch of the nervous system is in overdrive. The heart races, the breath is shallow, the muscles are tense. We are in a state of fight or flight, ready to confront or escape a perceived threat. When we are in a state of hypoarousal, the dorsal vagal branch of the nervous system takes over. The heart rate slows, the breath becomes shallow, the body goes limp. We are in a state of freeze or collapse, a state of shutdown in the face of overwhelming threat. For many with complex trauma, life is a relentless oscillation between these two extremes, with very little time spent in the gentle, restorative state of social engagement, governed by the ventral vagal branch.

The nervous system doesn't respond to what you believe. It responds to what it senses.

This is why relaxation can feel so threatening. To a nervous system that is accustomed to the high-octane intensity of hyperarousal or the numb emptiness of hypoarousal, the gentle hum of the ventral vagal state is a foreign language. It is a signal that the system does not know how to interpret. And so, it defaults to its old, familiar interpretation: danger. The body senses the shift, the letting go, the softening, and it sounds the alarm. It is a tragic and beautiful testament to the body’s fierce loyalty to its own survival. It is a love letter written in the language of fear. As we learn to gently and patiently introduce the body to the sensations of the ventral vagal state, we are not just relaxing. We are teaching the nervous system a new language. We are teaching it that safety is not just the absence of danger, but a presence in its own right. A presence that can be felt, that can be trusted, that can, eventually, become home. This is the heart of the work we do when we explore the Polyvagal Theory in practice.

The Ghost in the Machine

Complex trauma is not just an event, or even a series of events. It is a fundamental disruption in the fabric of our being. It is a ghost in the machine of the self, a silent and persistent presence that shapes our thoughts, our feelings, our very perceptions of the world. This ghost is not a metaphor. It is a physiological reality, a set of deeply ingrained neural pathways and bodily responses that were forged in the crucible of overwhelming experience. And this ghost has one primary directive: to ensure that we are never, ever that vulnerable again.

This is why relaxation can feel like such a betrayal. It is a betrayal of the ghost, a betrayal of the part of us that has sworn to protect us at all costs. To relax is to let down our guard, to open ourselves up to the possibility of connection, of intimacy, of joy. But to the ghost, these are not opportunities. They are risks. They are invitations for the other shoe to drop, for the past to repeat itself. And so, the ghost whispers its warnings, it tightens its grip, it floods the body with the familiar and strangely comforting sensations of anxiety and dread. It is a love story gone terribly wrong, a protector who has become a prison guard.

In my years of working in this territory, I have come to see this ghost not as an enemy to be vanquished, but as a lost and lonely part of the self, a part that is frozen in time, forever replaying the horrors of the past. The work, then, is not to fight the ghost, but to befriend it. To sit with it, to listen to its stories, to offer it the compassion and the understanding it never received. As the great spiritual teacher Sadhguru often points out in his discussions on the mechanics of the mind, the true self is not the whirlwind of thoughts and emotions, but the still, silent space in which they arise. By cultivating this inner space, this capacity for witnessing awareness, we can begin to create a container for the ghost, a place where it can finally, after all these years, begin to rest. We can learn to differentiate between the voice of the ghost and the voice of our own intuition, to honor the ghost’s protective intent without being ruled by its fear. This is the delicate art of befriending the inner critic.

The Seduction of Stillness

There is a profound and often overlooked difference between relaxation and stillness. Relaxation is a state of release, a letting go of tension. Stillness, on the other hand, is a state of presence, a deep and abiding connection to the present moment. For a nervous system that has been shaped by complex trauma, relaxation can feel like a freefall, a terrifying descent into the unknown. Stillness, however, can be a lifeline. It is a way of being with the intensity of our experience without being consumed by it. It is a way of finding the eye of the storm, the quiet center in the midst of the chaos.

Stillness is not something you achieve. It's what's already here beneath the achieving.

This is not the stillness of dissociation, of checking out, of numbing the pain. This is a vibrant, alive stillness, a stillness that is pregnant with sensation, with awareness, with life itself. It is the stillness of the mountain, which remains unmoved by the storms that rage around it. It is the stillness of the deep ocean, which is untouched by the turmoil on the surface. This is the stillness that we can learn to cultivate through practices like meditation, breathwork, and somatic inquiry. It is a stillness that is not dependent on external conditions, a stillness that we can carry with us into the heart of our daily lives.

When we can find this stillness within ourselves, the need for the armor of tension begins to fall away. We no longer need to brace ourselves against the world, because we have found a place of refuge within. We no longer need to fear the intensity of our own experience, because we have learned that we are vast enough to contain it all. This is not a quick fix, not a magic bullet. It is a slow, patient, and often messy process of re-wiring the nervous system, of teaching the body a new way of being in the world. It is the work of a lifetime, the great and glorious work of coming home to ourselves. It is the journey from a life of reaction to a life of conscious response.

The Unfolding Path

There is no map for this journey, no one-size-fits-all solution. The path of healing from complex trauma is a deeply personal one, a path that unfolds one step, one breath, one moment of courageous presence at a time. It is a path that is paved not with grand gestures and dramatic breakthroughs, but with the small, consistent, and often invisible acts of turning towards ourselves with kindness and with curiosity. It is the act of choosing, again and again, to meet our own experience with a gentle and unwavering attention.

And so the real work lies. Not in the endless analysis of the past, not in the desperate search for a cure, but in the simple and profound act of being with what is. Being with the tightness in the chest, the knot in the stomach, the tremor in the hands. Being with the fear, the grief, the rage, the shame. Not to fix it, not to change it, but simply to be with it. To offer it the gift of our presence, the warmth of our awareness. This is the alchemy of healing. This is how the lead of our suffering is transformed into the gold of our wisdom.

The algorithm of your attention determines the landscape of your experience.

As we walk this path, we begin to discover that we are not, in fact, broken. We are not damaged goods. We are, and have always been, whole. The trauma did not break us. It simply created a series of veils, of illusions, that have obscured this fundamental truth from our view. The work of healing is the work of gently and patiently lifting these veils, of remembering the wholeness that has been here all along. It is the work of reclaiming the parts of ourselves that we have been forced to abandon, of integrating the fragmented pieces of our experience into a new and more coherent whole. It is the sacred and essential work of becoming who we truly are. For those on this path, it can be helpful to explore the concept of the return to wholeness.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why does my body tense up when I try to meditate?

This is a common and understandable experience for those with a history of complex trauma. Meditation, in its essence, is an invitation to be still and to turn our attention inward. For a nervous system that is wired for danger, this can feel like a profound threat. The stillness can be interpreted as a state of vulnerability, and the inward focus can bring us into contact with the very sensations and emotions that we have spent a lifetime trying to avoid. The tensing of the body is a protective response, a way of bracing against this perceived threat. The key is not to fight this tension, but to meet it with a gentle and curious awareness. To notice it, to acknowledge it, and to allow it to be there without judgment. Over time, as the nervous system begins to learn that it is safe to be still, this tension will begin to soften.

Is it possible to ever feel truly safe in my body again?

Yes, it is. But it is important to understand that the path to a felt sense of safety is not a linear one. It is a journey of spirals, of ups and downs, of two steps forward and one step back. There will be moments of profound connection and safety, and there will be moments when the old fears and tensions resurface. This is not a sign of failure. It is simply the nature of the healing process. The key is to continue to cultivate the resources that support a felt sense of safety—practices like grounding, orienting, and co-regulation with a trusted other. And to remember that every moment of safety, no matter how fleeting, is a victory. It is a new neural pathway being forged, a new possibility being written into the story of your body.

How can I tell the difference between relaxation and dissociation?

This is a crucial distinction to make. Relaxation is a state of embodied presence. It is a feeling of being connected to your body, to your breath, to the present moment. There is a sense of warmth, of softness, of ease. Dissociation, on the other hand, is a state of disconnection. It is a feeling of being checked out, of being numb, of being distant from your own experience. It is a protective mechanism, a way of escaping from overwhelming sensations or emotions. One of the key differences is the quality of your awareness. In a state of relaxation, your awareness is clear and present. In a state of dissociation, your awareness is fuzzy, foggy, or absent altogether. Learning to track your own internal state, to notice the subtle shifts between presence and disconnection, is a vital skill on the healing path.

What if I can't even imagine what it would feel like to be relaxed?

This is a very real and valid experience for many survivors of complex trauma. When the nervous system has been in a state of chronic activation for years, or even decades, the concept of relaxation can feel completely alien. It can be like trying to imagine a color you have never seen. In this case, the work is not to try to force a state of relaxation, but to start with something much smaller, much more accessible. The work is to begin to cultivate moments of “less tension.” To notice the subtle shifts in your body, the moments when the grip of the armor loosens just a little bit. To savor these moments, to magnify them, to let them land in your awareness. This is how we begin to build a new vocabulary in the body, a new set of experiences that can, over time, grow into a genuine and sustainable capacity for rest. And for those who want to explore this further, I invite you to connect with the work we do at Kalesh.love, where we delve deeper into the mechanics of consciousness and the path to embodied liberation.

The journey out of the shattered armor of complex trauma is not a race to a finish line, but a slow and tender homecoming. It is the process of unlearning a lifetime of bracing, of vigilance, of waiting for the other shoe to drop. It is the radical act of teaching an exhausted nervous system that the war is, finally, over. That it is safe to lay down its weapons, to rest its weary head, to feel the warmth of the sun on its skin. It is the quiet miracle of returning to the body, not as a battlefield, but as a sanctuary. A place of wisdom, of resilience, of profound and unwavering grace.

Do the thing your body has been asking for.
